participants.name: A (sde4eg)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-sde4egro
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-sde4egro
participants.professional_persona: A 26-year-old freelance AI consultant who works with small retailers to deploy off-the-shelf AI tools. She is driven by the belief that every business, regardless of size, should benefit from AI.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Competent in cloud-based AI services, chatbot integration, and data visualisation. Holds a professional certificate in AI from a local polytechnic.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To build a consultancy that exclusively serves heartland enterprises and to develop a replicable 'AI for Micro-Business' toolkit.

participants.name: I (vhx7ev)
participants.professional_persona: I am a generative AI specialist at a creative agency, building custom image and text models for marketing campaigns. I transitioned from graphic design after completing a bootcamp in deep learning.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in diffusion models, GANs, and prompt engineering. I also fine-tune pre-trained models to match a brand's visual and tonal style.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to pioneer ethical generative AI in advertising, avoiding deepfake misuse. In the long run, I hope to teach AI creativity courses at local polytechnics.

participants.name: Dr. (80wla6)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Hafiz is a Research Fellow at Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD) specialising in human-AI collaboration. He actively participates in hackathons and ideathons organised by AISG to identify industry pain points.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Competent in interactive machine learning, user-centered design, and natural language processing. Proven ability to co-create solutions with non-technical stakeholders.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to build a community of practice on human-AI collaboration in Southeast Asia. Wants to mentor early-career researchers through company-led training programmes like AIAP.

participants.name: A (iq2sz6)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-iq2sz629
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-iq2sz629
participants.professional_persona: A 57-year-old retired teacher turned community volunteer who runs digital literacy workshops for seniors. He worries about AI-driven scams and wants to help the elderly stay safe online.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong communication and teaching skills, basic digital literacy, and familiarity with common online fraud tactics. Attends AI safety talks organised by IMDA.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To co-create a simple, AI-powered scam-detection tool for seniors and train 1,000 elderly residents in safe AI usage.

participants.name: A (7otjwe)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-7otjweua
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-7otjweua
participants.professional_persona: A 33-year-old sustainability manager at a real estate developer who wants to use AI to optimise building energy consumption. She sees the AI for public good principle as aligned with Singapore’s green goals.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in energy auditing, green building certifications, and IoT sensor deployment. Recently completed a micro-credential in AI for sustainability.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To implement AI-driven energy management across the company’s portfolio and later advise the government on AI for climate action.

initial_posts.narrative: As a generative AI specialist working in a creative agency, I see the NAIS 2.0 inputs as a solid foundation for accelerating AI adoption among enterprises like the ones I collaborate with. The S$500 million total funding is a clear signal of government commitment, and the S$330,000 cap per 100E project is generous enough to cover compute and talent costs for most mid-size AI experiments. Based on my experience with GPU-intensive model training, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours will be allocated to support the 100E programme and related initiatives—this aligns with typical cloud compute budgets for a national programme of this scale. For trainers, I assume about 50 AI experts will be assigned, drawing from my bootcamp experience where a small team of instructors can effectively guide dozens of practitioners. These inputs are realistic for the 2023 window, as the programme is still ramping up.
evidence: The policy context states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I take total_funding_sgd as 500,000,000.
The resource logic explicitly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so max_co_funding_per_project_sgd is 330,000.
Based on typical cloud GPU pricing and the scale of 100E projects (each requiring hundreds to thousands of hours), 500,000 GPU hours is a reasonable annual allocation for the programme's first year.
From my training experience, a team of 50 trainers can effectively support multiple cohorts of enterprises and practitioners, especially with online resources and structured curricula.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and 50 trainers, I estimate that in 2023 the programme will conduct about 80 enterprise outreach sessions. This is based on my experience that a small team of trainers can run roughly 1-2 sessions per week, targeting small and medium enterprises that need hands-on guidance. For researcher-enterprise pairings, I expect around 30 matchmaking events, as each event can pair multiple researchers with companies, but the limited number of trainers and the need for careful matching will keep the count moderate. Training workshops will number about 40, since each trainer can lead roughly one workshop per month, and the initial focus is on quality over quantity. Platform onboarding assists will be around 200, as each of the 50 trainers can assist about 4 enterprises in getting set up on the AI experimentation platform, reflecting the early-stage ramp-up and the need for personalized support. These estimates align with my view that the programme is starting cautiously to build momentum.
evidence: With 50 trainers and a focus on quality outreach, conducting 1-2 sessions per week per trainer yields about 80 enterprise outreach sessions in a 40-week active period.
Researcher matchmaking events require careful pairing; 30 events allow for meaningful connections without overwhelming the limited trainer and researcher pool.
Training workshops at one per trainer per month over 10 months gives 40 workshops, balancing depth of training with available personnel.
Platform onboarding assists of 200 reflect each trainer helping 4 enterprises, a realistic caseload for personalized technical support in the initial phase.

initial_posts.narrative: As a Research Fellow at SUTD who has participated in AISG hackathons and ideathons, I see the outputs phase as a direct consequence of the activities and inputs. With 80 outreach sessions, 30 matchmaking events, 40 training workshops, and 200 platform onboarding assists, the programme is actively engaging enterprises and researchers. Given the S$330,000 co-funding cap and 500,000 GPU hours, I estimate that about 80 projects will be approved in 2023, as each project requires substantial compute and funding, and the matchmaking and outreach efforts are still in early stages. From my experience, not all approved projects will yield a prototype in the same year; I estimate 60 prototypes will be developed, considering typical project timelines and the need for iterative refinement. The AI Apprenticeship Programme, with 40 workshops and 50 trainers, can support about 150 trainees completing the programme, assuming each trainer mentors 3-4 trainees over the year.
